text: **Tales of Sweet Dew** by Yuan Jiao **Table of Contents** - Wei Xiansheng - Su'e - Tao Xian - Lanzan - Nie Yinniang - Wei Zou - Yuan Guan - Hongxian - Xu Yunfeng **Wei Xiansheng** Wei Xiansheng was born during the Zhou Dynasty and lived in the Song region. Beyond Confucian texts, he studied musical compositions. In the early years of the Sui Dynasty, he traveled to the region west of the pass, where the Grand Music Master was examining music. The debate was not settled, and upon hearing that Mr. Wei had arrived, they all went to consult him. Mr. Wei then took the instruments from the conquered Chen Dynasty and, together with the music officials Su Kui and Cai Ziyuan, carefully examined their pitch and rhythm. Then, the percussion, strings, and wind instruments were all properly tuned. He was appointed to the Qing Shang Shu (Office of Music and Entertainment) as a Taile Guan (High-ranking Music Official) and received 200 pieces of silk as payment. Mr. Wei did not re-enter public service and returned to the Song region, entertaining himself with music and wine. By the end of the Sui Dynasty, civil war broke out, and Yang Xuangan was defeated. His chief strategist, Li Mi, fled to Yanmen, changing his name and beginning to teach. Mr. Wei was from the same hometown and, therefore, they began to visit each other. They often discussed the principles of music, and Li Mi was quite skilled. Mr. Wei then teased him, saying, "I see that you are dejected and confused, your mind is unsettled, and your speech is restrained. Dejection means recent defeat, confusion means having no support, an unsettled mind means an unsettled spirit, and restraint in speech means thinking of seeking help from others."

Now, they are hunting down the Pushe Party. Could it not be a respected elder?" Li Gong was startled and rose, taking the gentleman's hand and saying, "If you can understand me, can you not also teach me?" The gentleman replied, "You lack the grandeur of a ruler, the strategic vision of a general, but are a hero of chaotic times." Li Gong said, "Explain my actions and retreat accordingly." The gentleman continued, "A ruler encompasses heaven and earth, sets an example for all time. On the outside, daily use is unnoticed; within, yearly achievements are self-established. Emperor Yao consulted the Four Peaks and appointed Gun, who was later executed on Mount Yu, which was a demonstration of impartiality; Emperor Han relied on the Three Heroes and captured Liu Bang at Gaixia, which was also a demonstration of impartiality. Therefore, the phoenix has claws but does not use them, the unicorn has hooves but is forever discarded, because they can find their way and seek to gather themselves in the right time. This is the grandeur of a ruler. All generals set up the Taiyi flag, lead an army without fighting, and punish the guilty. The carved spear is handed out, the jade bow is drawn, indeed, what Fu Ji said is true, and Ji Liang is still here. Therefore, they focus on feasts and rewards, wait for the enemy to tire, cultivate their fields, and act when the opportunity arises. Thus, the wind rises and tigers roar, their power cannot be resisted; clouds gather and dragons rise, their momentum cannot be stopped. Confucius said, 'I will conquer in battle.' Mencius said, 'Who can be my enemy?' This is the talent of a general. There are those who use their talents and strategies. Publicly, they are generals for the state; privately, they are called rebels."

If one is selfish, they will surely seize wealth and women, and destroy cities. Zhu Hai was a guest of honor, and Fan Qi was an esteemed visitor. To hear the Way in the morning and die content, Gong Sun ultimately failed in his town. "I would rather betray others than be betrayed by them," Cao Cao could not conquer the world. To forget the gift of a thousand gold pieces, but remember the favor of a single meal, there are those who are grateful, but no one who wants to return home. Moreover, the *Rites of Zhou* say "to measure virtue," and the *I Ching* says "to wait for the right time." Even so, one must still consult others and cannot benefit oneself. Heaven and humanity are weary of chaos, and the mandate of heaven has its rightful heir. When timely rain falls, evil omens disappear; when the sun rises, thick ice melts. To bind a tiger with a rope, one cannot hope for the gate of a swift horse; to rush into water with a jar, it is not a safe place to live. I once hoped that a sage would arise in Fen and Jin, and I could serve him, thereby achieving wealth and honor. Li Gong brushed his robe and said, "The Sui Dynasty seized power through usurpation and murder, and my family holds a prominent position due to virtue and merit. With a single shout, the hearts of the people will respond; with a timely campaign, what can we not achieve? If the Way is right, we can take the four seas; if it is not, we can still rule one region. To entrust oneself to someone is something I truly cannot bear. You are a mere scholar, not capable of planning affairs." Thus, he rejected Hui Sheng. Because of this, Li Gong wrote a poem expressing his thoughts, which was discovered by local officials. He fled and gathered troops. He relied on Liyang to the north and Luo Kou to the south, with a million camps, competing with Wang Shichong. For two years, he was eventually defeated and overthrown. Reflecting on Hui Sheng's advice, he immediately surrendered to the Tang and was appointed as the Director of Agriculture. He then joined the rebellion at Tao Lin.

Hui Sheng, a person who has attained spiritual enlightenment, whose name is unknown, is a relative of Wen Zhen. Su'e, the concubine of Wu Sansi, was initially given to him as a concubine named Yau Niang, who was skilled in music and dance. Wu Sansi had a keen understanding of music and rhythm, and he considered Yau Niang to be the most extraordinary beauty of the world. Not long after, Yau Niang drowned in the Luo River, and Wu Sansi subsequently destroyed the entire family of Qiao. Someone then recommended Su'e, saying, "She is the daughter of a woman named Song from the Fengyang Gate of Xiangzhou, and she is skilled in playing the five-stringed instrument, the most extraordinary beauty of the world." Wu Sansi then sent three hundred pieces of silk to her as a gift. Su'e arrived, and Wu Sansi was very pleased, so he held a grand banquet to present Su'e. All the officials and nobles attended, except for the chancellor Di Renjie, who excused himself by claiming illness. Wu Sansi was angry and spoke about it at the banquet. After the banquet, someone told Di Renjie about this. The next day, Di Renjie apologized to Wu Sansi, saying, "I was suddenly stricken by illness yesterday and was unable to attend the summons. However, not seeing the beautiful lady is also my loss. If there are any future banquets, I will certainly arrive in advance." Su'e heard this and said to Wu Sansi, "Chancellor Di is a strong and resolute person, not someone who is familiar or intimate. Why should we force our own nature? If there is another banquet, you can choose not to invite Chancellor Di." Wu Sansi replied, "If you prevent my banquet, I will destroy his family."

After several days, a banquet was held again. The guests had not yet arrived. Lord Liang indeed arrived first. Wang Shu specially invited Lord Liang to sit in the inner chamber, they drank wine slowly, waiting for all the guests. They asked to bring out Su E first to briefly see her art, then stopped the wine and set up a couch to summon her. After a while, a servant came out and said, "Su E is hiding, and her whereabouts are unknown." Wang Shu went in to summon her, but saw nothing. Suddenly, in a crack in the innermost part of the hall, they heard the fragrance of orchids and musk. So they put their ears close to listen, and recognized it as Su E's voice. As fine as a strand of silk, barely able to recognize and distinguish, she said, "Ask the lord not to summon Lord Liang, now that he has indeed summoned him, I will not be reborn." Wang Shu asked for the reason, and Su E explained that she was not an ordinary spirit but a flower and moon demon sent by God to stir Wang Shu's heart with many words, intending to raise the Li family. Now that Lord Liang is a righteous man of the time, she dares not show herself. She once served as a servant or concubine, how could she be without emotion? She hopes the lord serves Lord Liang diligently and does not harbor other intentions. Otherwise, the Wu family will have no descendants left. After speaking, further questioning also did not receive a response.

San Si met Renjie and claimed that Su'e had a sudden illness, making it impossible for her to come out. Renjie did not know the reason for the ritual of showing respect. The next day, San Si secretly reported the matter to the emperor, who sighed and said, "What is granted by heaven cannot be discarded." Tao Xian was the grandson of Pengze. During the Kaiyuan era, he lived in Kunshan and was wealthy in land and property. He chose family members who were honest and capable and entrusted them with responsibilities, while he himself traveled on the rivers and lakes, exploring the misty waters extensively. He often stayed away for several years. When he saw his descendants grow up, he initially could not distinguish their names. Tao Xian's literary talent could be used for practical purposes. He considered himself unrestrained and did not plan to seek official positions. From birth, he was proficient in the eight musical tones. He ordered potters to make tiles and secretly recorded the seasons, tapping to get their sounds without losing their accuracy. He wrote eight chapters of "Music Record" to determine the correctness of the eight tones. He made three boats himself, which were extremely sturdy and clever. One boat for himself, one for guests, and one for food and drink. Guests included the former scholar-official Meng Yanshen, the scholar-official Meng Yunqing, and the commoner Jiao Sui, each with servants and concubines to accompany them. Tao Xian had a troupe of female musicians playing the clear merchant music. When encountering something interesting, he would explore the scenery until he had enough. Tao Xian was famous at court, and there was peace in the world. Whenever passing through cities and towns, he was always invited. Tao Xian refused and said, "I am a person from the wild, not a guest of high officials." There were also those who invited him without being asked. It depended on the character of the local official and the beauty of the landscape. People from Wu and Yue called him the "Water Immortal."

Once there was a relative who served as the governor of Nanhai. He visited the Shao Stones and went to see him. The governor was pleased by his distant visit and gave him a million coins as a gift, along with an ancient sword about two feet long and a jade ring with a diameter of four inches. There was also a昆仑slave named Mohe from a sea vessel, who was skilled in swimming and brave and agile. He then took all the gifts back and said, "These are the three treasures of my family." When returning on the boat, they landed at Bai Mang and entered the Xiang River. Whenever they encountered beautiful water, they would throw the ring and sword into the water and order Mohe to retrieve them, using it as a game and laughter. They did this for several years. When crossing Chaohu Lake, they also threw the ring and sword into the water and ordered Mohe to retrieve them. Mohe just entered the water, retrieved the sword and ring, and jumped out of the water, saying, "I was bitten by a poisonous snake." He quickly cut off a finger and thus was saved. Jiao Shui said, "Was Mohe's injury not due to the anger of some spirit? When the rhinoceros candle was lit, it was indeed his enemy. It seems that the water palace does not want people to spy on it." Xian said, "I have broken my promise. However, I have always admired the character of Xie Kangle, who said he would ultimately find joy in dying among the mountains and waters. But following one's preferences, knowing nothing else, and living temporarily in an inn, living on the vast earth, living in humble attire, enjoying the pleasures of the elite, living a wandering life and enjoying oneself for nearly thirty years, this is my fate; unable to ascend the jade steps and see the emperor, to serve and benefit the people, achieving one's lifelong aspirations, this is also my fate." Then he ordered the boat to move and said, "To bid farewell to the landscapes of Xiangyang before retiring to Wu Prefecture."

Traveling along the Western Saddle Mountain, he anchored the boat at a Buddhist temple called Jixiang. Seeing the river water black and stagnant, he said, "There must be a monster below." He then threw the ring and sword, ordering Moksha to go down and retrieve them. Seeing Moksha submerged in the waves, he remained there for a long time before surfacing, his strength perilously exhausted, barely able to hold on. He said, "The ring and sword cannot be retrieved. There is a dragon about two zhang tall, with the ring and sword placed in front of it. I reached out to take them, and the dragon glared angrily." Xian said, "You and the ring and sword are my three treasures. Now that we have lost the ring and sword, what good are you? You must fight for me." Reluctantly, Moksha let his hair fly and shouted loudly, his eyes bleeding from the corners. Once he entered the depths, he never came out again. After a long time, he saw Moksha's limbs torn apart, floating on the water, as if showing something to Xian.

Xian wept at the waterside and then ordered the boat to turn back. He composed a poem to narrate his own story, no longer discussing traveling the rivers and lakes. The poem reads: The old business of Mount Lu has its own master, and a new home in Wu and Yue is where I will live this life. With a few strands of white hair, I have not yet been able to return; a glance at the green mountains still makes my original plan stand. Crows roost on maple leaves as the sunset moves, and herons stand on reed roots with the autumn water clear. From now on, where will the boat go? The wine banners and song fans are welcoming. Meng Yanshen again served in the palace and was appointed as the magistrate of Wuchang; Meng Yunqing was then considered a top literary figure in the Southern Dynasties; Jiao Sui, during the Tianbao era, was a drinking enthusiast in Chang'an, and at that time, a poem called "The Eight Immortals of the Wine Cup" was written, which says, "Jiao Sui only becomes truly remarkable after drinking five dou of wine, and his eloquent and powerful debates astonish all four sides of the banquet." Lazy and Residual Ming Zan, also known as "Lazy and Residual," was a monk who performed menial tasks at Hengshan Temple during the early Tianbao era. After finishing his work, he would collect the leftovers and eat, being lazy and eating what was left over, hence his nickname "Lazy and Residual." During the day, he dedicated himself to the temple's tasks, and at night, he would rest under the cows, never showing any signs of fatigue, having done so for twenty years.

At that time, Duke Li Bi of Yeh was studying in a temple, observing what Lanzan was doing, and said, "He is no ordinary being." He listened to Lanzan's chanting in the middle of the night, which echoed through the mountains and forests. The Duke of Yeh was quite knowledgeable about music and could discern joy and sorrow, and he said, "Lanzan's chanting began with sorrow and ended with joy, indicating that he must be a fallen being whose time is nearly over." Waiting until midnight, the Duke of Yeh secretly went to pay his respects, standing outside the door and announcing his name before bowing. Lanzan shouted loudly and spat into the air, saying, "You are going to betray me!" The Duke of Yeh became even more respectful, bowing only. Lanzan was stirring the fire made from cow dung and eating a yam. After a while, he said, "You may sit on the ground." He took half of the yam he was eating and gave it to the Duke of Yeh. The Duke of Yeh received it with both hands and ate it, thanking him. Lanzan told the Duke of Yeh, "Be careful not to speak too much; you will become a prime minister for ten years." The Duke of Yeh bowed once and left. After a month, the governor went to worship at Mount Hua, observing the rituals very strictly. Suddenly, in the middle of the night, there was thunder and lightning, and a peak collapsed. The path along the mountain was blocked by a large boulder. They used ten oxen to pull it and hundreds of people to push it with shouts, but despite all their efforts, the stone remained immovable. There was no other way to proceed with the rituals. Lanzan said, "I don't need human effort; I will try to move it myself." Everyone laughed, thinking he was crazy. Lanzan said, "Why should you mock me? Just try it and see." The monks laughed but agreed to let him try. He stepped on the stone and moved it, and it suddenly turned and fell, making a sound like thunder. The mountain stone opened, and all the monks prostrated themselves, and the entire region hailed him as a saint, and the governor treated him like a god. Lanzan quietly decided to leave.

Tigers and leopards suddenly gathered outside the temple, causing daily killings and injuries, with no way to stop them. Lanzan said, "Give me a stick, and I will drive them all away for you." Everyone said, "If a large stone can be moved, tigers and leopards should be easy to control." They then gave him a branch of thorns, and everyone followed to watch. As soon as he stepped out, he saw a tiger carrying him away. After Lanzan left, tigers and leopards also disappeared. Later, Li Gong indeed became prime minister after ten years. Nie Yinniang Nie Yinniang was the daughter of Nie Feng, a general of Wei Bo during the reign of Emperor Dezong. When she was ten years old, a nun came to beg food at Nie Feng's house and saw Nie Yinniang, who liked her. The nun said, "I ask you to take this girl and teach her." Nie Feng was very angry and scolded the nun. The nun said, "Even if you lock her in an iron chest, she will still be stolen." That night, Nie Yinniang was indeed missing. Nie Feng was greatly alarmed and ordered people to search for her, but there was no trace of her. Her parents often thought about her and cried together.

Five years later, the nun sent Yin Niang back, telling Feng, "The training is complete; you can take her back." The nun then disappeared. The family was filled with a mix of sorrow and joy, and they asked her about her training. She replied, "At first, I only read scriptures and recited spells; there was nothing else." Feng did not believe her and pressed her for more details. Yin Niang said, "If I tell the truth, Father might not believe me; what should we do?" Feng responded, "Just tell the truth." She then said, "At first, I was taken away by the nun, and I did not know how far we traveled. When it was light, we arrived at a large stone cave, which was hollow and extended for dozens of steps. It was silent and uninhabited, with many monkeys and apes, and the pines and vines were even more deep and dense. The nun already had two daughters, each ten years old, who were intelligent and beautiful. They did not eat and could fly and walk on steep cliffs, like agile apes climbing trees without any falls or mistakes. The nun gave me a pill and ordered me to hold a precious sword, about one or two feet long, which was sharp enough to cut through hair. She then ordered the two daughters to teach me to climb, and I gradually felt as light as the wind. After one year, I practiced hunting monkeys and apes, and I never missed. Later, I practiced hunting tigers and leopards, and I would sever their heads and return. After three years, I could fly, and I was sent to hunt eagles and falcons, and I never missed. The blade of the sword gradually shortened by five inches, and when flying or walking, no one could see where I went or came from. By the fourth year, the two daughters were left to guard the cave, and she took me to the city—I did not know where it was. Those who pointed at the person counted their faults one by one, saying, 'Kill them and bring their heads back, without them knowing.'"

The person was told to steady their courage, as it would be as easy as catching a flying bird. They were given a sheep's horn dagger with a blade three inches wide and proceeded to assassinate someone in broad daylight in the city square, unseen by others. They returned with the head in a bag, and then used a drug to dissolve it into water. Five years later, they were told, "A certain high-ranking official has committed a crime and has wrongfully harmed several people. You can enter his room at night and bring back his head." They took the dagger and entered the room, finding no obstacles through the door. They hid on the beam and, at dusk, retrieved the head. The nun was furious and asked, "Why was it so late?" The person replied, "I saw the man playing with a child, who was adorable, and I hesitated to act." The nun scolded, "From now on, when you encounter such people, you must first sever what they love, and then carry out the act." The person bowed and thanked her. The nun said, "I will open the back of your head to hide the dagger," and did so without causing any harm. When needed, the dagger could be drawn. She said, "Your skills are now complete, you may return home." She then sent them back, saying, "You will not see me again for twenty years." Feng heard these words and was very afraid. Later, at night, they would disappear and return by morning. Feng dared not question them and, as a result, did not show much affection. One day, a young man who sharpened mirrors arrived at the door and said, "This person can be my husband." Feng told their father, who, though reluctant, had no choice but to agree. Feng was then married to him. Her husband could only sharpen mirrors and had no other skills. Their father provided them with abundant food and clothing and gave them a separate room to live in.

After several years, her father passed away. The Hui commander recognized her exceptional abilities and appointed her as a clerk in his inner office with gold and silk. In this way, another several years passed until the Yuanhe period. The Hui commander and the governor of Chenxu, Liu Changyi, did not get along, and he sent Yin Niang to assassinate him. Yin Niang declined the order and went to Xu. Liu Changyi, skilled in divination, already knew she was coming. He summoned the military commander and gave him orders: "The next morning, go to the northern part of the city early and wait for a man and a woman each riding a white and black horse. When they arrive at the gate, if a magpie comes and crows, the man will try to shoot it with a bow but miss. His wife will take the bow and shoot the magpie with one shot. Bow to them and say: 'I wish to meet you, so I have come from afar to greet you.'" The military commander followed the orders and met them. Yin Niang and her husband said, "Governor Liu is indeed a divine person, otherwise, how could he summon us? We wish to see Governor Liu." Liu greeted them, and Yin Niang and her husband bowed and said, "We have offended Governor Liu and deserve to die a thousand deaths." Liu said, "No, it is natural for people to be loyal to their own masters. What is the difference between Wei and Xu now? Please stay here and do not doubt us." Yin Niang replied, "Governor Liu, you have no one on your side. I wish to leave him and join you. I admire your divination skills." Because she knew that the Hui commander was not as capable as Liu. Liu asked what she needed, and she said, "Every day, I only need two hundred coins." He agreed to her request. Suddenly, the two horses disappeared. Liu sent someone to look for them, but they could not find where they went. Later, he found two paper horses in a cloth bag, one black and one white.

A month or so later, Yin Niang told Liu, "They do not know when to stop and will surely send someone else to follow. Tonight, I will ask to cut my hair, tie it with red silk, and send it to the bed of the Wei commander as a sign that I will not return." Liu listened to her. Until the fourth watch of the night, she then returned, saying, "I have sent her message. That night, they will surely send the assassin named Jingjing to kill me and the head of the enemy's chief. At this time, I will also find a way to kill him, please do not worry." Liu was open-minded and generous, showing no fear. That night, the candle was lit, and after midnight, indeed there were two flags, one red and one white, fluttering as if striking each other at the four corners of the bed. After a long time, she saw a person fall from the air, body and head separated. Yin Niang also came out, saying, "Jingjing is already dead." She dragged him out to the courtyard, with the powdered medicine turned him into water, and his hair and beard no longer existed. Yin Niang said, "The next night, they will send the skilled assassin named Kongkong to follow. Kongkong's divine skills are such that no one can see how he uses them, and no ghost can follow his tracks. He can enter from emptiness into the void, becoming invisible and disappearing."

The Skills of Hidden Maiden, thus cannot reach her realm. This is the fortune of the Shè. But wrap her neck with Yutian jade and cover her with a quilt, Hidden Maiden will transform into a tiny insect and secretly enter the Shè’s intestines to listen and observe. There is no place left to escape.” Liu followed what was said. By the third watch, his eyes were not yet closed, he indeed heard a sharp sound from his neck, very loud. Hidden Maiden leaped out from Liu’s mouth and said, “The Shè has no more worries. This person is like a swift falcon, if one strike fails, he will fly away far, ashamed of not hitting the target. In less than an hour, he was already a thousand miles away.” Afterward, when he looked at the jade, there was indeed a mark where the dagger had been drawn, more than several inches deep. From then on, Liu treated her with greater respect and gifts. In the eighth year of Yuanhe, Liu went from Xu to pay homage. Hidden Maiden did not want to go, saying, “From now on, I will seek mountains and rivers, visit sages.” But she asked for provisions for her husband. Liu followed the agreement. Later, she gradually disappeared without a trace. When Liu died in the army, Hidden Maiden also rode a donkey to the capital, wept at the coffin, and then left. In the year of Kai Cheng, Zhang Zong, the son of Zhang Yì, was appointed as the governor of Lingzhou, and on his way to Shu along the栈道, he met Hidden Maiden. She looked as she did before, very happy to see him, still riding her white horse as usual. She told Zhang Zong, “You are in great danger, it is not suitable for you to be here.” She gave him a pill and told him to swallow it, saying, “Next year, you must urgently leave your post and return to Luoyang to escape this disaster. My medicine will only protect you for one year.” Zhang Zong did not believe it very much. He gave her silk and brocade, but Hidden Maiden did not accept anything, and just got drunk and left.

The following year, even if he had not resigned from his position, he indeed died in Lingzhou. From then on, nobody saw Lady Yin anymore. Wei Zou Wei Zou was a master of the five tones and skilled in long-distance calls, referring to himself as "The Gentleman Beyond the Crowd." He took the imperial examination to become a Jinshi but stopped after failing once, saying, "A man's ambition to travel and serve in various places, how can he be bound by the mundane affairs of the world?" He traveled to Yueyang, where the prefect appointed him due to their personal acquaintance. After several months, he resigned citing illness. Wei Zou's younger brother Wei Lei drowned in Dongting Lake while on a boat journey. Wei Zou cried bitterly at the water's edge and moved his boat to the temple of the lake deity, intending to burn it down. He said, "A wealthy merchant was safe and sound, but my brother, poor and frail, met this disaster. What use is your temple to me?" Suddenly, he fell asleep in the boat and dreamed of a deity in formal attire coming to visit him. The deity said to Wei Zou, "On the path of the afterlife, there are no wrongful killings. Your father, a former city commander, was upright and brave, so even ghosts and spirits avoided him. He removed many excessive shrines, but two should not have been abolished. These two deities appealed to the emperor, who initially refused. They persistently requested for over ten years, and finally, the emperor agreed to allow one of his descendants to thank the two deities for the temples that were abolished. However, it must be someone who cannot understand the way when they step back and cannot contribute to the times when they move forward, hence your wise brother is the one. If the search for his body fails, it will be my fault, and I will command the water workers to send his body to the lake." Wei Zou woke up in shock, and the matter was quickly resolved. He then ordered fishing boats to set hooks and lines, and indeed found his brother's body on the shore.

That night, the man dreamed again of a god who thanked him, saying, "Ghosts and gods do not fear anger, but they fear decisiveness, because it stems from sincerity. You are now so decisive, that is why I am afraid. In the past, when the music was played at Dongting Lake, it was under my jurisdiction. I wish to offer the finest music as a return for your great kindness. I hope that by viewing the rhythms of Xianchi, you can be freed from the worries of the world." Suddenly, he saw golden and jade instruments, and the sounds were clear and stirring. The driver was greatly astonished and thought it was not based on anything. When the music ended, he woke up. Yuan Guan Yuan Guan was a monk at Huilin Temple in Luoyang at the end of the Dali period. He was capable of managing his fields and was wealthy in grain and silk. Beyond Buddhist studies, he was well-versed in music theory. People called him 'the wealthy monk,' but no one knew where the name came from. Li Janyi Yuan was the son of a high-ranking official. During the Tianbao era, he was devoted to feasting and drinking. His father, Zhang, was a governor and was captured by rebels, so he gave up his fine clothes and moved to Huilin Temple. He gave all his family's property to the temple, and the temple provided him with a bowl and a cup of food each day. He did not keep servants, cut off all communication, and only had a deep friendship with Yuan Guan, sitting close together and talking quietly from morning till night. People criticized them for being so different, saying they were not fitting together.

For thirty years, the two gentlemen agreed one day to travel to Shu Prefecture, reaching Qingcheng and Emei Mountains, to seek the Dao and medicines. Yuan Guan wished to travel to Chang'an, passing through Xie Valley; Li Gong wished to go to Jingzhou and the Three Gorges. They debated these two routes for half a year without reaching a decision. Li Gong said, "I have already renounced worldly affairs, how could I choose the route through the two capitals?" Yuan Guan said, "The journey is not entirely within one's control, please let us leave through the Three Gorges." So they set out from Jingjiang to the Three Gorges. They arrived at Nanpu, mooring their boat under a mountain, and saw several women, dressed in brocade, carrying jars to draw water. Yuan Guan saw them and wept, saying, "I did not wish to come here, fearing I would encounter these women." Li Gong was surprised and asked, "Since coming up the Three Gorges, there have been many such people, why are you particularly afraid of these few?" Yuan Guan said, "Among them, the pregnant woman surnamed Wang is the place where I will be reborn. She has been pregnant for over three years without giving birth, because I have not yet come. Now that I have seen her, my fate has a destination. This is what the Buddha teaches as 'reincarnation.'" He continued, "Please use a spell to hasten her birth, and let us briefly stop our boat to bury me under this mountain. Bathe the child for three days, and you should visit. If we look at each other and smile, it will be my recognition of you. Twelve years later, on the night of the Mid-Autumn Festival, outside the Tianzhu Temple in Hangzhou, will be the time we meet again."

Li Gong eventually regretted his journey and wept bitterly. He summoned the woman and informed her of the medical text. The woman returned home joyfully. Soon after, all the relatives gathered and offered a dried fish at the water's edge. Li Gong went to give her a red talisman. Yuan Guan was prepared with a bath and new clothes. That night, Yuan Guan died and the pregnant woman gave birth. Three days later, Li Gong went to see the new baby, who smiled when shown to the light. Li Gong wept and fully informed the king. The king then spent much of his wealth to bury Yuan Guan. The next day, Li Gong returned by boat, speaking of returning to Huilin. He asked about Yuan Guan's family and learned that he had already passed away. Twelve years later, in the eighth month of autumn, Li Gong went directly to Yuhang to fulfill his appointment. At that time, the sky at Tianzhu Temple had just cleared after the rain, and the moonlight filled the river, making it impossible to find anyone. Suddenly, he heard a herdsman singing a "Zhu Zhi Ci" while sitting on a cow and playing its horn, with a topknot and short clothes. Soon, the herdsman arrived at the temple, and it was Yuan Guan. Li Gong approached and asked, "Is Master Yuan well?" Yuan Guan replied, "You are a true believer. Our paths are different, so be careful not to get close. His worldly ties are not yet complete, but I hope you will continue to cultivate and not fall short, and we will meet again." Li Gong, unable to speak with him, looked at him and wept. Yuan Guan sang another "Zhu Zhi Ci," moving forward step by step, and even over long distances, the sound of the singing could still be heard. The words were sharp and the melody high, and no one knew where it went. As he first arrived at the temple, he sang: On the stone of three lives, the old soul enjoys the moon and sings in the wind, without discussing it. Feeling ashamed that a lover has come from afar, though the body may change, the spirit remains the same.

Before the temple, another song was sung: The affairs before and after one’s life are vast and uncertain; to talk about karmic connections might break one’s heart. Having traveled extensively through the landscapes of Wu and Yue, I return by boat on misty waters to Qutang Gorge. Three years later, Li Gong was appointed as a Censorial Adviser; one year after that, he passed away. **Red Thread** Red Thread, a concubine of Xue Song, the military governor of Lu Prefecture, was skilled in playing the Ruan Xian and well-versed in the classics and history. Xue Song entrusted her with drafting official documents and titled her “Inner Secretary.” During a grand banquet in the army, Red Thread said to Xue Song, “The sound of the Jiegu drum is quite melancholic; the one tuning it must have something on their mind.” Xue Song, who also understood music, replied, “As you say.” He then summoned the person and asked them. They replied, “My wife passed away last night; I dare not ask for leave.” Xue Song immediately sent them home. At that time, after the chaos of the An Lushan Rebellion, the two rivers (Yellow River and Huai River) were still unsettled. The Zhaoyi Army was initially established with Fuyang as its base, and Xue Song was tasked with holding it, controlling the eastern region. After the devastation of war, the military government was in its early stages. The court also arranged for Xue Song’s daughter to marry the son of Tian Chengsi, the military governor of Wei and Bo, and the son of Tian Chengsi married the daughter of Ling Hu Zhang, the military governor of Hua Prefecture. The three regions were interlinked by marital ties, with envoys and messengers traveling daily among them. At that time, Tian Chengsi had been suffering from a severe heat-related illness, which worsened in the summer. He often said, “If I could move my base to the eastern region, where it is cooler, I could prolong my life by several years.” He then recruited soldiers who were ten times more courageous, gathering three thousand of them, whom he called “Outer Household Men” and treated with great care. He always kept three hundred of them stationed at his residence. He chose a good day to plan an attack on Lu Prefecture. Upon hearing this, Xue Song was filled with anxiety and worry, muttering to himself, with no solution in sight.

At night, when the water clock was about to strike, the gates of the courtyard were already closed. The master held a walking stick and walked in the courtyard, with only Red Line accompanying him. Red Line said, "The master has been preoccupied for a month, with no time to rest or eat. Is he worried about something in the neighboring prefecture?" The master replied, "This matter concerns the safety of the state, which is beyond your understanding." Red Line said, "Although I am of lowly status, I also have the ability to relieve the master's worries." The master then told her everything, saying, "I have inherited my grandfather's legacy and received great favor from the state. If I lose this land, the centuries-old achievements of my ancestors will be lost forever." Red Line said, "It is easy for me; it does not warrant the master's concern. I request to be allowed to go to Wei Prefecture to observe the situation and see if there is any opportunity. I will leave at the first watch and return by the third watch. Please prepare a horse for me and a letter of greeting. The rest will be arranged upon my return." The master was greatly surprised, saying, "I did not know you were a supernatural being; it is my ignorance. However, if the mission fails, it will bring more trouble. What should I do?" Red Line said, "My mission will always succeed." She then entered her room, prepared her belongings, combed her hair into a black bun, adorned it with golden wind hairpins, wore a purple embroidered short coat, and tied on light green shoes. She wore a dragon-patterned dagger at her chest and inscribed the sacred name of the Great One on her forehead. After bowing twice, she suddenly disappeared.

Sōng turned around and closed the door, then sat upright with his back to the candle. Usually, he drank no more than a few cups, but on that night, he raised his cup and drank over ten without becoming drunk. Suddenly, he heard the morning horn echoing through the wind; a single leaf fell, glistening with dew. Startled, he rose and inquired, and at that moment, Hóngxiàn returned. Sōng greeted her with joy and asked, "Did the matter succeed?" She replied, "I dared not dishonor your command." He then asked, "Was there any killing or harm?" She answered, "Not to that extent—only the golden box by the bed was taken as proof."

Redline said, "At three hours before midnight, I arrived at Hui Prefecture, passing through several doors and reaching the sleeping quarters. I heard the noise of men sleeping in the corridors, their snores loud as thunder. I saw soldiers moving in the courtyard, creating a bustling atmosphere. I opened the left door and reached the bedchamber. The old man, Master Tian, was deeply asleep in the bedchamber, his head resting on a headrest made of fine rhinoceros horn, his hair adorned with yellow silk. On the pillow, there was a sheath containing a seven-star sword. Above the sword, there was an open gold box. Inside the box were the birth year and zodiac sign of the person, along with the names of the Big Dipper stars, and there were also fine incense and precious pearls scattered on top. In the luxurious tent, he hoped to be free of worries before his death; in the fragrant room, he did not realize that his life was in the hands of the protagonist. There was no need to capture him; it only added to the sorrow. At that moment, the candlelight was dim, and the incense in the brazier was nearly extinguished. Guards and weapons were arranged around. Some were snoring with their heads touching the screen, others were sleeping and stretching with fans in hand. I grasped the hairpins and pulled the robe, making the old man act as if sick or drowsy, unable to wake up. I then took the gold box and returned. After leaving the western gate of Hui Prefecture, I traveled two hundred miles, seeing a high copper platform and the Zhang River flowing eastward. The morning wind stirred the fields, and a slanted moon was in the forest. Worries left, and joy returned, and I forgot the journey. I expressed gratitude and repaid kindness, fulfilling the expectations of my heart. Therefore, I traveled three hours past midnight, covering over seven hundred miles, entering a dangerous country, passing through five or six cities, hoping to alleviate the ruler's worries, and daring to speak of the hardships."

Sung then sent an envoy to deliver a letter to Chengsi, which read: "Last night a guest came from Wei, saying he obtained a golden box from the head of the general. He did not dare to keep it, so he respectfully returned it sealed." The special envoy rode like a star and arrived at midnight, seeing the search for the golden box, the entire army was worried and doubtful. The envoy knocked on the door with a whip and requested to see him out of turn. Chengsi hurriedly came out, and the envoy handed him the golden box. When he received it, he was shocked and fell to the ground. He then kept the envoy in his residence, treated him with hospitality, and gave him many gifts. The next day, he sent an envoy to deliver thirty thousand bolts of silk, two hundred famous horses, and other items of similar value to Sung, saying: "My life and death depend on your kindness. I should know to repent and reform, and I will no longer bring trouble upon you. I will obey your commands and dare to suggest marriage; I will serve as a follower and guide when you come. The servants I have called 'outer house men' were originally set up to guard against theft and not for any other purpose. Now I have released them from their duties and sent them back to their fields." As a result, within a few months, envoys from both north and south of the Yellow River came to visit, and Red Line decided to leave. Sung said, "You were born in my family, and now where do you wish to go? Moreover, I am still relying on you, how can you even consider leaving?" Red Line replied, "In my past life, I was a man who traveled and studied in the rivers and lakes, reading divine medical books to save people from disasters. At one time, there was a pregnant woman in our village who suddenly contracted a toad sickness. I gave her a potion made with wolfberry flowers, and she and her two unborn children all died. In one act, I killed three people. Because of my dark deeds, I was punished and reincarnated as a woman, living in a lowly position, with a star of thievery in my destiny."

I am fortunate to have been born into public service, and it has been nineteen years now. I have lived in luxurious silk robes, feasted on the finest delicacies, and been honored with extra favor, achieving a high degree of glory. Moreover, the state has established a stable and just government, bringing boundless blessings. These rebels, however, have violated the natural order and must be eliminated. Yesterday, I went to Hui Jun to show my gratitude. By doing so, I have preserved the cities and saved the lives of ten thousand people; I have made the traitorous ministers fear and the loyal heroes feel secure in their plans. As a woman, my contributions have been significant, and I can certainly redeem my past sins and return to my true form. I shall then retreat into the world, settle my mind beyond material concerns, purify my vital energy, and achieve immortality. Suo said, "Not so. I leave you a thousand gold coins for sustenance in the mountains." Redline replied, "This concerns the afterlife; how can one plan for it?" Knowing that Redline could not stay, Suo arranged a grand farewell banquet, gathering all the guests, and held a night banquet in the main hall. Suo sang a song to toast Redline, and asked the guest Cold Yang to compose a poem. The poem read: A song of sorrow for the lotus picker in a *Mulan* boat, sending the guest off from a hundred-foot tower, leaving the soul weary. Like the goddess of the Luo River riding the mist away, the blue sky is boundless, and the water flows empty.

After the song ended, Song was overwhelmed with sorrow. Hongxian tore her sleeve and wept, then pretended to be drunk and left her seat, disappearing without a trace. Xu Yunfeng, a musician who played the flute. At the beginning of the Zhenyuan era, Wei Yingwu was appointed as the governor of Hezhou from his position as Lang of the Lantai, but it was not what he had hoped for, and he was quite disheartened. He sailed eastward in a small boat and docked at Lingbi Post Station at night. The sky was cloudy and the first autumn dew had formed, making it cold. He recited poems on the boat, intending to compose a piece. Suddenly, he heard the sound of Xu Yunfeng's flute and sighed for a long time. Wei Gong was well-versed in music theory and said that the sound of the flute was very similar to that of Li Mu, who played the legal music of the Pear Garden during the Tianbao period. He then summoned Xu Yunfeng and asked him, and Xu Yunfeng replied, "I am the grandson of Li Mu." Xu Yunfeng said, "I am an old scholar from Rencity, who has not returned for many years. In the first year of the Tianbao era, I was born a month after the eastern pilgrimage. When the imperial procession returned, they stopped at Rencity. My grandfather heard that I had been born and was very pleased, so he carried me to Scholar Li Bai and asked him to give me a name. Li Gong was sitting at the Flag Pavilion, loudly ordering wine. The lady of the wine shop, Lady Helan, who was nearly ninety years old, invited Li to drink on the upper floor, and my grandfather played the flute to send the wine. Li Gong, drunk and holding a writing brush, wrote on my chest, 'The person under the tree does not speak; not speaking is truly good. If one speaks at noon, the mist will disperse and the old treasures will be revealed.' My grandfather said, 'I originally asked the scholar for a name, but I do not understand what he wrote.' Li Gong said, 'This is the name right here. The person under the tree is wood and son; wood and son, the character for Li. Not speaking is to not say; not saying, the character for Mu. Good is a woman; a woman, the character for grandson.'

The translation of the source text is as follows: When it is noon, it is said to be "noon" (日中), which is a pun on the name "Xu" (许). The phrase "cloud emerging from a sealed place" (云出封中) is another pun that leads to the name "Yunfeng" (云封). Thus, the person is Xu Yunfeng, the grandson of Li Mu. Later, he was given this name. At the age of ten, he found himself alone, and so he rode a symbolic horse and traveled west to Chang'an. His maternal grandfather felt compassion for his long journey and ordered him to study with his uncles. He believed that Xu had a natural talent for music and taught him to play the transverse flute. Whenever a new piece was completed, his grandfather would pat his back and praise him. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, in the fourteenth year of the Tianbao era (755) in June, the emperor stayed at Mount Lishan, which was the birthday of the imperial concubine Yang Guifei. The emperor ordered the small musical group to perform at the Hall of Eternal Life and they played a new piece that did not yet have a name. Just then, litchi fruit was brought from the South Sea, and the new piece was named "Lizhi Xiang" (Litchi Fragrance). The people around the emperor cheered loudly, and the sound echoed through the mountains. That year, An Lushan rebelled, and the emperor returned to the capital. After that, Xu experienced many hardships and turmoil, spending nearly forty years drifting in the South Sea. Recently, he visited his relatives and was about to arrive at Longqiu.

"Lord Wei said, 'I have a child of my wet nurse, named Thousand Gold. He once studied the flute under Li Gongfu during the Tianbao era. His artistry was perfected, but he died young, and I often grieve for him. This old flute was a gift from Master Li.' He then took out the old flute. Yunfeng knelt, holding the flute with deep sorrow, and examined it, saying, 'This is indeed a fine flute, but it was not played by my maternal grandfather.' He then told Lord Wei, 'The bamboo grows in the south of Yunmeng, and its quality is judged at the Ko Pavilion. It should be harvested before the seventh day of the seventh month this year and the seventh day of the seventh month next year. If harvested too late, its sound becomes dull; if harvested too early, its sound becomes light and insubstantial. If the bamboo is too superficial, it is dry within; if it is dry, it does not fully absorb the vital energy; if it does not fully absorb the vital energy, the bamboo is flawed. To fully express a single note requires nine breaths. In ancient times, the perfect sound was achieved with a flute that had twelve joints, each with twelve beats, which is the pinnacle of music today. For example, the melody of "Falling Plum Blossoms" evokes the feelings of travelers at Jin Valley, and the melody of "Breaking Willow Branches" conveys the sorrow of soldiers at the Jade Pass. There is indeed a clear and unique sound, but only the perfect sound can invoke the divine and bring blessings. This flawed bamboo will break when exposed to the perfect sound, which is why it was not played by my maternal grandfather.' Lord Wei said, 'If you wish to prove your discernment, the flute breaking will cause no harm.' Yunfeng then took the flute and began to play "The Six States Ode." Before the first section was finished, the flute suddenly split in the middle. Lord Wei was astonished for a long time, and then he honored Yunfeng in the music department."

《甘泽谣》袁郊 目录 魏先生 素娥 陶岘 懒残 聂隐娘 韦驺 圆观 红线 许云封 魏先生 魏先生生于周，家于宋；儒书之外，详究乐章。隋初出游关右，值太常考乐，议者未平，闻先生来，竞往谒问。先生乃取平陈乐器，与乐官苏夔、蔡子元等详其律度，然后金石丝竹，咸得其所。内致清商署为太乐官，敛帛二百段以酬之。先生不复入仕，遂归梁宋，以琴酒为娱。 及隋末兵兴，杨玄感战败，谋主李密亡命雁门，变姓名以教授。先生同其乡曲，由是遂相来往。常论钟律，李密颇能，先生因戏之曰：“观吾子气沮而目乱，心摇而语偷。气沮者，新破败；目乱者，无所依；心摇者，神未定；语偷者，思有谋于人。

今方捕蒲山党，得非长者乎？”李公惊起，执先生手曰：“既能知我，岂不能教我欤？”先生曰：“吾子无帝王规模，非将相才略，乃乱世之雄杰耳。”李公曰：“为吾辨析行藏，亦当由此而退。” 先生曰：“夫为帝王者，包罗天地，仪范古今。外则日用而不知，中则岁功而自立。尧询四岳，举鲧而殛羽山，此乃出于无私也；汉任三杰，纳良而围垓下，亦出于无私也。故凤有爪吻而不施，麟有蹄足而永废者，能得其道而求自集于时。此帝王之规模地。凡为将军者，幕建太一旗，驱无战之师，伐有罪之民，乃雕戈既授，玉弩斯张，诚负羁之有言，那季良之犹在。所以务其宴犒，致逸待劳，修其屯田，观衅而动。遂使风生虎啸，不可抗其威；云起龙骧，不可攘其势。仲尼曰：‘我战则克。’孟轲云：‘夫谁与敌。’此将帅之才也。至有秉其才知，动以机钤．公于国则为帅臣，私于己则曰乱盗。

私于己，必掠取财色，屠其城池。朱亥为前席之宾，樊期为升堂之客。朝闻夕死，公孙终败于邑中；宁我负人，曹操岂兼于天下？是忘辇千金之贶，报陈一饭之恩，有感谢之人，无怀归之众。且鲁史之诫曰‘度德’，连山之文曰‘待时’，尚欲谋于人，不能惠于己。天人厌乱，历数有归。时雨降而妖祲除，太阳升而层冰释。引绳缚虎，难希飞兔之门；赴水持罂，岂是安生之地？吾尝望汾、晋有圣人生，能往事之，富贵可取。”李公拂衣而言曰：“隋氏以篡杀取天下，吾家以勋德居人表。振臂一呼，众心响应；提兵时伐，何往不下？道行，可以取四海；不行，亦足以王一方。委质于人，诚所未忍。汝真竖儒，不足以计事。”遂绝魏生。 因写怀赋诗，为乡吏发觉，李公脱身而走，所在收兵。北依黎阳而南据洛口，连营百万，与王世充争衡。首尾二年，终见败覆。追思魏生之说，即日遂归于唐，乃授司农之官，复构桃林之叛。

魏生，得道之士，亡其名，盖文贞之宗亲也。 素娥 素娥者，武三思之姬人也。三思初得乔氏青衣窈娘，能歌舞。三思晓知音律，以窈娘歌舞天下至艺也。未几，沉于洛水，遂族乔氏之家。左右有举素娥者曰：“相州风阳门宋媪女，善弹五弦，世之殊色。”三思乃以帛三百段往聘焉。 素娥既至，三思大悦，遂盛宴以出素娥。公卿大夫毕集，唯纳言狄仁杰称疾不来。三思怒，于座中有言。宴罢，有告仁杰者。明日，谢谒三思，曰：“某昨日宿疾暴作，不果应召。然不睹丽人，亦分也。他后或有良宴，敢不先期到门。”素娥闻之，谓三思曰：“梁公强毅之士，非款狎之人，何必固抑其性。若再宴，可无请召梁公也。”三思曰：“倘阻我宴，必族其家。”

后数日，复宴。客未来。梁公果先至。三思特延梁公坐于内寝，徐徐饮酒，待诸宾客。请先出素娥，略观其艺，遂停杯设榻召之。 有顷，苍头出曰：“素娥藏匿，不知所在。”三思自入召之，皆不见。忽于堂奥隙中闻兰麝芬馥，乃附耳而听，即素娥语音也，细于属丝，才能认辨，曰：“请公不召梁公，今固召之，某不复生也。“三思问其由，曰：“某非他怪，乃花月之妖。上帝遣来，亦以多言荡公之心，将兴李氏。今梁公乃时之正人，某固不敢见。某尝为仆妾，宁敢无情？愿公勉事梁公，勿萌他志。不然，武氏无遗种矣。”言讫，更问亦不应也。

三思出见仁杰，称素娥暴疾，未可出。敬事之礼，仁杰莫知其由。明日，三思密奏其事，则天叹曰：“天之所授，不可废也。” 陶岘 陶岘者，彭泽之孙也。开元中，家于昆山，富有田业。择家人不欺而了事者悉付之，身则泛艚江湖，遍游烟水，往往数岁不归。见其子孙成人，初不辨其名字也。 岘之文学，可以经济；自谓疏脱，不谋宦游。有生之初，通于八音，命陶人为甓，潜记岁时，敲取其声，不失其验。撰《乐录》八章，以定八音之得失。自制三舟，备极坚巧。一舟自载，一舟置宾，一舟贮饮馔。客有前进士孟彦深、进士孟云卿、布衣焦遂，各置仆妾共载。而岘有女乐一部，奏清商曲。逢奇遇兴，则穷其景物，兴尽而行。岘且闻名朝廷，又值天下无事，经过郡邑，无不招延，岘拒之曰：“某麋鹿间人，非王公上客。”亦有未招而自请者，系方伯之为人，江山之可驻耳。吴、越之士，号为“水仙”。

曾有亲戚，为南海守，因访韶石，遂往省焉。郡守喜其远来，赠钱百万，遗古剑长二尺许，玉环径四寸，海舶昆仑奴名摩河——善泅水而勇捷。遂悉以所得归，曰：“吾家之三宝也。” 及回棹，下白芒，入湘江，每遇水色可爱，则遗环剑于水，令摩诃下取，以为戏笑也。如此数岁。 因渡巢湖，亦投环剑而令取之。摩诃才入，获剑环，跳波而出焉，曰：“为毒蛇所啮。”遽刃去一指，乃能得免。焦遂曰：“摩诃所伤，得非阴灵为怒乎？犀烛下照，果为所仇。盖水府不欲人窥也。”岘曰：“敬奉渝矣。然某尝慕谢康乐之为人，云终当乐死山水间，但徇所好，莫知其他。且栖迟于逆旅之中，载于大块之上，居布素之贱，擅贵游之欢，浪迹怡情垂三十年，固其分也；不得升玉墀，见天子，施功惠养，得志平生，亦其分也。”乃命移舟，曰：“要须一别襄阳山水，后老吴郡也。”
行次西塞山，泊舟吉祥佛舍，见江水黑而不流，曰：“此下必有怪物。”乃投环剑，命摩诃下取。见摩诃汩没波际，久而方出，气力危断，殆不任持，曰：“环剑不可取。有龙高二丈许，而环剑置前。某引手将取，龙辄怒目。”岘曰：“汝与环剑，吾之三宝。今者既亡环剑，汝将安用？必须为我力争也。”摩诃不得已，被发大呼，目眦流血。穷泉一入，不复出矣。久之，见摩诃肢体磔裂，浮于水上，如有示于岘也。

岘流涕水滨，乃命回棹。因赋诗自叙，不复议游江湖矣。 诗曰： 匡庐旧业自有主，吴越新居安此生。 白发数茎归未得，青山一望计还成。 鸦栖枫叶夕阳动．鹭立芦根秋水明。 从此舍舟何所诣？酒旗歌扇正相迎。 孟彦深复游青琐，出为武昌令；孟云卿当时文学乃南朝上品；焦遂，天宝中为长安饮徒，时好事者为《饮中八仙歌》云，云：“焦遂五斗方卓然，高谈雄辩惊四筵。” 懒残 懒残者，名明瓒，天宝初衡岳寺执役僧也。退食，即收所余而食，性懒而食残，故号“懒残”也。昼专一寺之功，夜止群牛之下，曾无倦色，已二十年矣。

时邺侯李泌寺中读书，察懒残所为，曰：“非凡物也。”听其中宵梵呗，响彻山林，李公情颇知音，能辨休戚，谓：“懒残经音先凄惋而后喜悦，必谪堕之人，时将去矣。”候中夜，李公潜往谒焉，望席门通名而拜。懒残大诟，仰空而唾曰：“是将贼我。” 李公愈加谨敬，惟拜而已。懒残正拨牛粪火，出芋啖之，良久乃曰：“可以席地。”取所啖芋之半以授焉。李公捧承就食而谢。谓李公曰：“慎勿多言，领取十年宰相。”公一拜而退。 居一月，刺吏祭岳，修道甚严。忽中夜风雷，而一峰颓下，其缘山磴道为大石所拦。乃以十牛縻绊以挽之，又以数百人鼓噪以推之，物力竭而石愈固；更无他途，可以修事。懒残曰：“不假人力，我试去之。”众皆大笑，以为狂人。懒残曰：“何必见嗤？试可乃已。”寺僧笑而许之。遂履石而动，忽转盘而下，声若震雷。山石既开，众僧皆罗拜，一郡皆呼“至圣”，刺史奉之如神。懒残悄然乃怀去意。

寺外虎豹忽尔成群，日有杀伤，无由禁止。懒残曰：“授我棰，为尔尽驱除之。”众皆曰：“大石犹可推，虎豹当易制。”遂与之荆梃，皆蹑而观之。才出门，见一虎衔之而去。懒残既去，虎豹亦绝踪。 后李公果十年为相也。 聂隐娘 聂隐娘者，贞元中魏博大将聂锋之女也。方十岁，有尼乞食于锋舍，见隐娘，悦之，乃云：“问押衙乞取此女教。”锋大怒，叱尼。尼曰：“任押衙铁柜中盛，亦须偷去矣。”后夜，果失隐娘所在。锋大惊骇，令人搜寻，曾无影响。父母每思之，相对啼哭而已。

后五年，尼送隐娘归，告锋曰：“教已成矣，可自领取。”尼欻亦不见。一家悲喜，问其所习。曰：“初但读经念咒，余无他也。”锋不信，恳诘，隐娘曰：“真说，父恐不信，如何？”锋曰：“但真说之。”乃曰：“隐娘初被尼挈去，不知行几里。及明，至大石穴中，嵌空数十步。寂无居人，猿猱极多，松萝益邃。尼先已有二女，亦各十岁，皆聪明婉丽，不食，能于峭壁上飞走，若捷猱登木，无有蹶失。尼与我药一粒，兼令执宝剑一口，长一二尺许，锋利，吹毛可断。遂令二女教某攀援，渐觉身轻如风。 一年后，刺猿猱；百无一失；后刺虎豹，皆决其首而归。三年后，能飞，使刺鹰隼，无不中。剑之刃渐减五寸，飞走遇之，亦莫知其去来也。至四年，留二女守穴，挈我于都市——不知何处也，指其人者，一一数其过，曰：‘为我刺其首来，无使知觉。

定其胆，若飞鸟之易也。’授以羊角匕首，刃广三寸，遂白日刺其人于都市中，人莫能见。以首入囊反命，则以药化之为水。 五年，又曰：‘某大僚有罪，无故害人若干，夜可入其室，决其首来。’又携匕首入其室，度其门隙，无有障碍。伏之梁上，至暝时，得其首归。尼大怒曰：‘何太晚如是？’某云：‘见前人戏弄一儿，可爱，未忍便下手。’尼叱曰：‘已后遇此辈，必先断其所爱，然后决之。’某拜谢。尼曰：‘吾为汝开脑后藏匕首。’而无所伤，用即抽之。曰：‘汝术已成，可归家。’遂送还，云：‘后二十年，方可一见。’” 锋闻语，甚惧。后遇夜即失踪，及明而返。锋已不敢诘之．因兹亦不甚怜爱。忽值磨镜少年及门，曰：“此人可与我为夫。”白父，又不敢不从．遂嫁之。其夫但能淬镜，余无他能，父乃给衣食甚丰，具外室而居。

数年后，父卒。魏帅知其异，遂以金帛署为左右吏。如此又数年，至元和间，魏帅与陈许节度使刘昌裔不协，使隐娘贼其首。隐娘辞帅之许，刘能神算，已知其来，召衙将，令曰：“来日早至城北，候一丈夫一女子各跨白黑卫，至门，遇有鹊来噪，丈夫以弓弹之，不中；妻夺夫弹，一丸而毙鹊者，揖之曰：吾欲相见，故远相祗迎也。”衙将受约束，遇之。隐娘夫妻云：“刘仆射果神人，不然者，何以动召也。愿见刘公。”刘劳之，隐娘夫妻拜曰：“得罪仆射，合万死。”刘曰：“不然。各亲其主，人之常事。魏今与许何异？请当留此，勿相疑也。”隐娘谢曰：“仆射左右无人，愿舍彼而就此。服公神明耳。”盖知魏帅之不及刘也。刘问所须，曰：“每日只要钱二百文足矣。”乃依所请。忽不见二卫所在，刘使人寻之，不知所向。后潜于布囊中见二纸卫，一黑一白。

后月余，白刘曰：“彼未知止，必使人继至。今宵请剪发，系之以红绡，送于魏帅枕前，以表不回。”刘听之。至四更，却返，曰：“送其信矣。是夜必使精精儿来杀某，及贼仆射之首。 此时亦万计杀之，乞不忧耳。”刘豁达大度，亦无畏色。是夜明烛，半宵之后，果有二幡子，一红一白，飘飘然如相击于床四隅。良久，见一人自空而踣，身首异处。隐娘亦出，曰：“精精儿已毙。”拽出于堂之下，以药末化之为水，毛发不存矣。 隐娘曰：“后夜当使妙手空空儿继至。空空儿之神术，人莫能窥其用，鬼莫得蹑其踪，能从空虚入冥漠，无形而灭影。

隐娘之伎，故不能造其境。此即系仆射之福耳。但以于阗玉周其颈，拥以衾，隐娘当化为蠛蠓，潜入仆射肠中听伺。其余无逃避处。”刘如言。至三更，瞑目未熟，果闻项上铿然，声甚厉。隐娘自刘口中跃出，贺曰：“仆射无患矣。此人如俊鹘，一搏不中，即翩然远逝，耻其不中耳。才未逾一更，已千里矣。” 后视其玉，果有匕首划处，痕逾数分。自此，刘转厚礼之。 自元和八年，刘自许入觐，隐娘不愿从焉，云：“自此寻山水，访至人。”但一一请给与其夫。刘如约。后渐不知所之。 及刘薨于军，隐娘亦鞭驴而一至京师，柩前恸哭而去。 开成年，昌裔子纵除陵州刺史，至蜀栈道，遇隐娘。貌若当时，甚喜相见，依前跨白卫如故。谓纵曰：“郎君大灾，不合适此。”出药一粒，令纵吞之，云：“来年火急抛官归洛，方脱此祸。吾药力只保一年患耳。”纵亦不甚信。遗其缯彩，隐娘一无所受，但沉醉而去。

后一年，纵不休官，果卒于陵州。自此，无复有人见隐娘矣。 韦驺 韦驺者，明五音，善长啸，自称“逸群公子”。举进士，一不第便已，曰：“男子四方之志，岂拘节于风尘哉！”游岳阳，太守以亲知见辟。数月，谢病去。 驺亲弟騋，舟行溺于洞庭湖，驺乃水滨恸哭，移舟湖神庙下，欲焚其庙。曰：“千金贾胡，安稳获济；吾弟穷悴，乃罹此殃。焉用尔庙为？”忽于舟中寐，梦神人盛服来谒，谓驺曰：“幽冥之途，无枉杀者。明公先君尝为城守，方刚谠正，鬼神避之。撤淫祠甚多，不当废者有二。二神上诉，帝初不许。固请十余年，乃许与后嗣一人，谢二废庙之主。然亦须退不能知其道、进无以补于时者，故贤弟当之耳。倘求丧不获，即我之过，当令水工送尸湖上。”驺惊寤，其事遽止。遂命渔舟施钩缗，果获弟之尸于岸。

是夕，又梦神谢曰：“鬼神不畏忿怒，而畏果敢，以其诚也。 君今为人果敢如是，吾所以怀畏。昔洞庭张乐，是吾所司，愿以至音，酬君厚惠。所冀观咸池之节奏，释浮世之忧烦也。”忽睹金石羽龠，铿锵振作。驺甚叹异，以为非据。曲终乃寤。 圆观 圆观者，大历末洛阳惠林寺僧。能事田园，富有粟帛。梵学之外，音律贯通。时人以“富僧”为名，而莫知所自也。李谏议源，公卿之子，当天宝之际，以游宴饮酒为务；父憕居守，陷于贼中，乃脱粟布衣，止于惠林寺，悉将家业为寺公财，寺人日给一器、食一杯饮而已。不置仆使，绝其闻知，惟与圆观为忘言交，促膝静话，自旦及昏。时人以清浊不伦，颇生讥诮。

如此三十年，二公一旦约游蜀州，抵青城、峨嵋，同访道求药。圆观欲游长安，出斜谷；李公欲上荆州、三峡。争此两途，半年未决。李公曰：“吾已绝世事，岂取途两京？”圆观曰：“行固不由人，请出三峡而去。”遂自荆江上峡。 行次南浦，维舟山下，见妇女数人，锦裆，负瓮而汲。圆观望见，泣下曰：“某不欲至此，恐见其妇人也。”李公惊问曰：“自上峡来，此徒不少，何独恐此数人？”圆观曰：“其中孕妇姓王者，是某托身之所，逾三载尚未娩怀，以某未来之故也。今既见矣，即命有所归。释氏所谓‘循环’也。”谓公曰：“请假以符咒，遣其速生，少驻行舟，葬某山下。浴儿三日，公当访临。若相顾一笑，即某认公也。更后十二年中秋月夜，杭州天竺寺外，与公相见之期。”

李公遂悔此行，为之一恸。遂召妇人，告以方书。其妇人喜跃还家。顷之，亲族毕至，以枯鱼献于水滨。李公往，为授朱字符。圆观具汤沐，新其衣装。是夕，圆观亡而孕妇产矣。 李公三日往观新儿，襁褓就明，果致一笑。李公泣下，具告于王。王乃多出家财，葬圆观。明日，李公回棹，言归惠林。询问观家，方知已有理命。 后十二年秋八月，直诣余杭，赴其所约。时天竺寺山雨初晴，月色满川，无处寻访。忽闻葛洪川畔有牧竖歌《竹枝词》者，乘牛叩角，双髻短衣。俄至寺前，乃圆观也。李公就谒曰：“观公健否？”却向李公曰：“真信士。与公殊途，慎勿相近。俗缘未尽，但愿勤修不堕，即遂相见。”李公以无由叙话，望之潸然。圆观又唱《竹枝》，步步前去，山长水远，尚闻歌声。词切韵高，莫知所诣。初到寺前，歌曰： 三生石上旧精魂，赏月吟风不要论。 惭愧情人远相访，此身虽异性常存。

寺前又歌日： 身前身后事茫茫，欲话因缘恐断肠。 吴越山川游已遍，却回烟棹上瞿塘。 后三年，李公拜谏议大夫；一年，亡。 红线 红线，潞州节度使薛嵩家青衣。善弹阮咸，又通经史，嵩遣掌笺表，号曰“内记室”。时军中大宴，红线谓嵩曰：“羯鼓之音颇凄，调其声者，必有事也。”嵩亦明晓音律，曰：“如汝所言。”乃召而问之。云：“某妻昨夜亡，不敢乞假。”嵩遽遣放归。 时至德之后，两河未宁，初置昭义军，以滏阳为镇，命嵩固守，控压山东。杀伤之余，军府草创。朝廷复遣嵩女嫁魏博节度使田承嗣男，男娶滑州节度使令狐彰女，三镇互为姻娅，人使日浃往来。 时田承嗣尝患热毒风，遇夏增剧，每曰：“我若移镇山东，纳其凉冷，可缓数年之命。”乃募军中武勇十倍者，得三千人，号“外宅男”而厚恤养之，常令三百人常直州宅。卜选良日，将并潞州。嵩闻之，日夜忧闷，咄咄自语，计无所出。

时夜漏将传．辕门已闭，杖策庭除，唯红线从行。红线曰：“主自一月，不遑寝食，意有所属，岂非邻境乎？”嵩曰：“事系安危，非尔能料。”红线曰：“某虽贱品，然亦有解主忧者。”嵩乃具告其事，曰：“我承祖父遗业，受国家厚恩，一旦失其土疆，即数百年勋伐尽矣。”红线曰：“易尔，不足劳主忧也。乞放某一到魏郡，看其形势，观其有无。今一更首途，三更可以复命。请先定一走马，兼具寒暄书，其他即俟某却回也。”嵩大惊曰：“不知汝是异人，我之暗也。然事若不济，反速其祸，奈何？”红线曰：“某之行，无不济者。”乃入闺房，饬其行具。梳乌蛮髻，攒金风钗，衣紫绣短袍，系青丝轻履，胸前佩龙文匕首，额上书太乙神名，再拜而倏忽不见。
嵩乃返身闭户，背烛危坐。常时饮酒，不过数合，是夕举觞，十余不醉。忽闻晓角吟风．一叶坠露，惊而起问，即红线回矣。嵩喜而慰问曰：“事谐否？”曰：“不敢辱命。”又问曰：“无伤杀否？”曰：“不至是，但取床头金盒为信耳。”

红线曰：“某子夜前三刻即到魏郡，凡历数门，遂及寝所。闻外宅男止于房廊，睡声雷动。见中军士卒步于庭庑，传呼风生。某发其左扉，抵其寝帐。田亲家翁止于帐内，鼓趺酣眠，头枕文犀，髻包黄毂。枕前露橐七星剑，剑前仰开一金盒，盒内书生身甲子与北斗神名，复著名香及美珠，散覆其上。扬威玉帐，但期心豁于生前；同梦兰堂，不觉命悬于手下。宁劳擒纵，只益伤嗟。时则蜡炬光凝，炉香烬煨，侍人四布，兵器森罗。或头触屏风，鼾而亸者；或手持巾拂．寝而伸者。某攀其簪珥，縻其襦裳，如病如昏，皆不能寤。遂持金盒以归。既出魏城西门，将行二百里，见铜台高揭，漳水东注，晨飙动野，斜月在林。忧往喜还，顿忘于行役；感知酬德，仰副于心期。所以夜漏三时，往返七百余里，入危邦，经五六城，冀减主忧，敢言其苦。”

嵩乃发使遗承嗣书曰：“昨夜有客从魏中来，云自元帅头边获一金盒，不敢留驻，谨却封纳。”专使星驰，夜半方到，见搜捕金盒，一军忧疑。使者以马挝叩门，非时请见。承嗣遽出，使者以金盒授之；捧承之时，惊怛绝倒。遂驻使者止于宅中，狎以宴私，多其赐赉。明日，遣使赍缯帛三万匹、名马二百匹，他物称是，以献于嵩，曰：“某之首领，系在恩私，便宜知过自新，不复更贻伊戚。专膺指使，敢议姻亲；役当奉毂后车，来则麾鞭前马。所置纪纲仆号为‘外宅男’者，本防他盗，亦非异图。今并脱其甲裳，放归田亩矣。” 由是，一两月内，河北、河南人使交至，而红线辞去。嵩曰：“汝生我家，而今欲安往？又方赖汝，岂可议行？”红线曰：“某前世本男子，游学江湖间，读神农药书，救世人灾患。时里有孕妇忽患蛊症，某以芫花酒下之，妇人与腹中二子俱毙。是某一举杀三人。阴功见诛，降为女子，使身居贱隶，气禀贼星。

所幸生于公家，今十九年矣。使身厌罗绮，口穷甘鲜，宠待有加，荣亦至矣。况国家建极，庆且无疆，此辈背违天理，当尽弭患。昨往魏郡，以示报恩。两地保其城池，万人全其性命；使乱臣知惧，烈士安谋。在某一妇人，功亦不小，固可赎其前罪，还其本形。便当遁迹尘中，栖心物外，澄清一气，生死常存。” 嵩曰：“不然，遗尔千金，为居山之所给。”红线曰：“事关来世，安可预谋？” 嵩知不可驻留，乃广为饯别，悉集宾客，夜宴中堂。嵩以歌送红线酒，请座客中冷朝阳为辞。辞曰： 采菱歌怨木兰舟，送客魂消百尺楼。 还似洛妃乘雾去，碧天无际水空流。

歌毕，嵩不胜悲，红线反袂且泣，因伪醉离席，遂亡其所在。 许云封 许云封，乐工之笛者。贞元初，韦应物自兰台郎出为和州牧，非所宜愿，颇不得志。轻舟东下，夜泊灵璧驿。时云天初秋，瀼露凝冷，舟中吟讽，将以属词。忽闻云封笛声，嗟叹久之。韦公洞晓音律，谓其笛声酷似天宝中梨园法曲李暮所吹者。遂召云封问之，乃是李暮外孙也。 云封曰：“某任城旧士，多年不归。天宝改元，初生一月。时东封回驾，次至任城。外祖闻某初生，相见甚喜，乃抱诣李白学士，乞撰令名。李公方坐旗亭，高声命酒。当垆贺兰氏，年且九十余，邀李置饮于楼上，外祖高笛送酒。李公握管醉书某胸前曰：‘树下人不语，不语真我好。语若及日中，烟霏谢陈宝。’外祖辞曰：‘本于学士乞名，今不解所书之语。’李公曰：‘此即名在其间也。树下人是木子；木子，李字也。不语是莫言；莫言，暮也。好是女子；女子，外孙也。

语及日中，是言午；言午，是许也。烟霏谢陈宝，是云出封中，乃是云封也。即李暮外孙许云封也。’后遂名之。某才始十年，身便孤立，因乘义马，西入长安。外祖悯以远来，令齿诸舅学业，谓某性知音律，教以横笛。每一曲成，必抚背赏叹。值梨园法部置小部音声，凡三十余人，皆十五以下。天宝十四载六月日，时骊山驻跸，是贵妃诞辰。上命小部音声乐长生殿，仍奏新曲，未有名。会南海进荔枝，因以曲名《荔枝香》。左右欢呼，声动山谷。是年安禄山叛，车驾还京。自后俱逢离乱，漂流南海近四十载。今者近访诸亲，将抵龙邱。”

韦公曰：“我有乳母之子，其名千金，尝于天宝中受笛李供奉，艺成身死，每所悲嗟。旧吹之笛，即李君所赐也。”遂囊出旧笛。云封跪捧悲切，抚而观之，曰：“信是佳笛，但非外祖所吹者。”又谓韦公曰：“竹生云梦之南，鉴在柯亭之下。以今年七月望前生，明年七月望前伐。过期不伐，则其音窒；未期而伐，则其音浮。浮者，外泽中干；干者，受气不全；气不全，则其竹夭。凡发扬一声，出入九息。古之至音者，一叠十二节，一节十二敲，今之名乐也。至如《落梅》流韵，感金谷之游人；《折柳》传情，悲玉关之戍客。诚有清响异音，非至音，无以降神而祈福也。其已夭之竹，遇至音必破，所以知非外祖所吹者。” 韦公曰：“欲信汝鉴，笛破无伤。”云封乃捧笛吹《六州遍》。 一叠未尽，划然中裂。韦公惊叹久之，遂礼云封于曲部。
